questions.question: Define contiguous memory allocation and explain its basic working principle.
keyPoints: Each process gets a single continuous block of memory
Memory is divided into partitions (fixed or variable)
Process remains in its allocated block until completion
Uses base and limit registers for protection

questions.question: Differentiate between internal fragmentation and external fragmentation with examples.
keyPoints: Internal: wasted space within allocated partition (fixed partitioning)
External: scattered free blocks that cannot satisfy large requests (variable partitioning)
Internal = allocated - actual used
External = sum of all small holes

questions.question: Explain the First Fit memory allocation algorithm with an example.
keyPoints: Scans memory list from beginning
Allocates first hole large enough for process
Fast allocation time
Example: holes [20KB, 30KB, 15KB], process 18KB gets 20KB hole

questions.question: What is memory compaction? How does it help in memory management?
keyPoints: Moves all free memory holes together
Creates one large contiguous free block
Solves external fragmentation problem
Disadvantage: requires process relocation and significant CPU time

questions.question: Explain the difference between fixed and variable partitioning in memory management.
keyPoints: Fixed: partitions created at system startup, fixed sizes
Variable: partitions created dynamically based on process size
Fixed: suffers internal fragmentation
Variable: suffers external fragmentation

questions.question: Describe the Best Fit and Worst Fit allocation algorithms. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Best Fit: smallest suitable hole, minimizes leftover space but slow
Worst Fit: largest hole, creates larger leftover but fast
Best Fit: more external fragmentation over time
Worst Fit: less efficient memory utilization

questions.question: Explain the concept of swapping in operating systems. When is it used?
keyPoints: Moving processes between main memory and secondary storage
Used when memory is full and new process needs space
Entire process is moved to disk (swap out) and back to memory (swap in)
Enables execution of processes larger than physical memory

questions.question: Discuss the various memory allocation algorithms (First Fit, Best Fit, Worst Fit, Next Fit) with their advantages, disadvantages, and time complexities.
keyPoints: First Fit: O(n), fastest, may leave large holes
Best Fit: O(n²), best memory utilization but slow
Worst Fit: O(n), prevents small holes but poor utilization
Next Fit: O(n), similar to First Fit but starting point varies
Each has trade-offs between speed and memory efficiency

questions.question: Explain the fragmentation problem in memory management. How does it occur in different allocation schemes? What are the solutions?
keyPoints: Fragmentation: wasted memory that cannot be used
Internal: occurs in fixed partitioning - unused space within partitions
External: occurs in variable partitioning - scattered small holes
Solutions: compaction (for external), optimal partition sizing
Compaction moves all free memory together at cost of CPU time

questions.question: A computer system has a memory of 100KB. Using contiguous memory allocation with variable partitions, show step-by-step memory allocation for the following process sequence: P1(30KB), P2(20KB), P3(40KB), P1 completes, P4(15KB), P5(25KB). Use First Fit algorithm.
keyPoints: Initial: single 100KB hole
P1(30KB): allocated at start, leaves 70KB hole
P2(20KB): first fit finds 70KB hole, leaves 50KB
P3(40KB): first fit finds 50KB hole, leaves 10KB
P1 completes: creates 30KB hole
P4(15KB): first fit finds 50KB hole, leaves 35KB
P5(25KB): first fit finds 35KB hole, leaves 10KB
Show final memory state with all holes and allocated blocks

questions.question: Explain memory protection in contiguous memory allocation. How are base and limit registers used? What happens when a process tries to access memory outside its allocated region?
keyPoints: Base register: starting address of process in memory
Limit register: size of the allocated partition
Hardware checks: every memory access validated against base and limit
If address < base OR address >= base + limit: TRAP generated
Prevents one process from accessing another's memory
Essential for multi-programming and system security
